title: podman login should store the credentials in the kernel keyring
body:
It should default to the keyring and then fall back to file storage. 

https://bugzilla.redhat.com/show_bug.cgi?id=1627819

comments.body: I will look into it and try to come back with a proposal :heavy_check_mark:

@rhatdan @mtrmac @umohnani8, I have doubts regarding the kernel keyring, mainly because the keyring is not namespaced. This means that `root-container-A` can read the credentials of `root-container-*` which is a blocker.

Docker uses the `pass(1)` if available on the host which is definitely a security improvement over a json file.

I don't see the kernel keyring as abig an issue as you. We are blocking access to it via SELinux and seccomp, and we can use usernamespace by default.    Could we use kernel keyring for the root less case. Since this is the one that is causing us issues.
Does the seccomp filter prevent the use of kernel keyrings altogether inside of the container?  If yes we probably should relax this if using a userns.

The upstream kernel has a PR to make kernel keyrings unigue per User Namespace, so this would allow us to use kernel keyring for non privileged users, since the container process would not be able to see it.  And the containers themselves could use a kernel keyring.
Awesome. Once that's in the kernel, we can continue working on it.
@vrothberg Anything on this, or should I open fhis up for others to look at?
@rhatdan, feel free to put others on this issue. The copy-detection mechanism received a higher priority but is not yet finished.

would it be helpful for me if I could see the bugzilla page....?
![bugz](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/23665824/56136852-98b55d80-5f61-11e9-90a9-7c161f3511cf.png)
